ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers beef meatball as a closest parent; no specific entry for Chinese-style seasoned beef balls exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Beef meat products) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific code for Chinese-style beef balls exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789004 covers meatball as a food concept; no beef-specific or Chinese-style variant code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Signature Beef Balls is 3 pieces, weighing approximately 90 g, as sold at Loong Dim Sum.

nutrient_source.title: Beef balls, boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo beef meatball entry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese-style seasoned beef balls exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for beef meatballs (cooked) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo beef ball category. Sodium is elevated to reflect typical Chinese-style seasoning (soy sauce, salt). Values represent a reasonable composite estimate per 100 g of cooked product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based foods and meatball analogues — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style beef balls. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of cooked meat-based products with minor starch binders, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of meat-dominant foods as low GI (typically 0–40). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 4.95) / 100 ≈ 1.5, where carbohydrate per 90 g serving = 5.5 × 0.9 = 4.95 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~468 mg per 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Chinese-style beef balls due to the high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef balls have a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content, making them a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as sweet sauces or rice noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~520 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate their intake of beef balls due to the combined effect of saturated fat and high sodium, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef balls (牛肉丸, niú ròu wán) are a classic Chinese dim sum and street food item made from finely minced or ground beef that is seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, and other aromatics, then shaped into firm, bouncy spheres and cooked by boiling or steaming. The texture is characteristically springy and dense, achieved through vigorous hand-beating or mechanical processing of the beef paste, which develops the myosin proteins and gives the balls their signature bite. In Singapore, beef balls are commonly served at dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and hotpot establishments, either as a standalone dish or added to soups and noodle broths. The Loong Dim Sum version is described as handcrafted with a specialty seasoning blend, suggesting artisanal preparation. Nutritionally, beef balls are a moderate-calorie, high-protein food (~17.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat content (~11 g per 100 g) and low carbohydrate content (~5.5 g per 100 g), making them a low-glycemic-index food suitable for blood sugar management. However, the sodium content is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to seasoning, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Beef balls are not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten due to potential starch binders. They are a culturally significant Cantonese and Teochew food item widely enjoyed across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese-style seasoned beef balls was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef meatball data and cross-referenced with HPB Foodo beef ball category. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database meat-product category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type (cooked seasoned beef meatball) is nutritionally well-characterised in approved sources, and the adaptation is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked beef meatball data and HPB Singapore Foodo beef ball category; no exact match for Loong Dim Sum Signature Beef Balls exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium is elevated to 520 mg per 100 g to reflect typical Chinese-style seasoning (soy sauce, salt, oyster sauce); this is consistent with HPB Foodo sodium ranges for seasoned beef balls. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: beef balls contain no vitamin C as this nutrient is not present in cooked meat products; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central beef meatball entries. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source for this product type. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. 9. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous meat-dominant food category from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Chinese beef balls was found in the approved GI literature. 10. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed from the field survey listing alone; the restaurant should be verified against MUIS Singapore halal certification. 11. Serving size of 90 g (3 pieces) is estimated based on typical dim sum beef ball weight of approximately 25–35 g per piece; actual weight may vary by restaurant.
